race.name: Cascade Crest
race.tagline: Fifty kilometers through the high Cascades of Oregon — a spring ski marathon at the foot of Mt Bachelor where the season ends on a summit note.

vitals.aid_stations: 3 stations with water, sports drink, food

climate.description: A late-season race at nearly 2,000 meters altitude. April brings longer days and strong sun, but the high elevation maintains snow quality. Temperatures range from -5°C to 10°C. Corn snow and spring crust are common, with mornings firm and afternoons soft.
challenges: Strong spring sun causes rapid snow softening by midday
UV exposure at nearly 2,000m requires sunscreen and eye protection
Klister wax conditions common in warm years
Morning crust can be icy and fast on descents

course.primary: Loops through the Mt Bachelor Nordic trail system and surrounding volcanic terrain. The high Cascades landscape features volcanic ridges, old-growth forest, and views of the Central Oregon peaks. Technical descents through forested sections test handling skills.
course.surface: Machine-groomed trails on volcanic terrain, some sections through open meadows above treeline
course.grooming: Good grooming maintained by Mt Bachelor Nordic Center, though spring conditions can be variable
features: High-altitude course on the flanks of Mt Bachelor (2,764m)
Views of Broken Top, South Sister, and the Central Oregon Cascades
Spring skiing with long daylight hours
Volcanic terrain creates unique rolling topography
One of the latest-season Nordic marathons in the US

nordic_lab_rating.score_note: High-altitude volcanic terrain provides a unique setting. Spring timing means variable snow but dramatic scenery. Small but dedicated field. Accessible from Bend, Oregon.

history.summary: Founded around 2010, the Cascade Crest ski marathon takes advantage of the deep snowpack and late-season conditions at Mt Bachelor in central Oregon. The race grew out of the region's vibrant Nordic community centered around Bend, Oregon. Mt Bachelor's Nordic center, operated by the Mt Bachelor Sports Education Foundation, provides the trail infrastructure. The event celebrates the end of the ski season with a spring marathon through one of the most scenic volcanic landscapes in the American West.
notable_facts: One of the latest-season Nordic marathons in the United States
Mt Bachelor receives an average of 460 inches (11.7 meters) of snow annually
The volcanic terrain of the Central Oregon Cascades creates unique course topography
Bend, Oregon is one of the fastest-growing Nordic skiing communities in the US
The race often coincides with the final weeks of the Mt Bachelor Nordic season
